speaker: interviewer
text: Penina, we left off where this teacher had guided you toward the selection that had you-- got you out of Auschwitz. But before we do that, perhaps there's something else about Auschwitz you might want to talk about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you, you asked if we worked. The only thing occ-- occasionally my sister was picked to work with-- the work consisted of chopping stones. We could never figure out what it was, but she would just go for that one day and chop stones and then come back. And then the only-- the work that we participated in was that they asked for volunteers to carry the food from the kitchen to the barracks. And we got our porridge and piece of bread. And we had these huge milk cans that two people had to go in the kitchen and get it and either the grass soup that we got or the porridge and to carry it to the barracks, and then help dish it out. And we always volunteered to do that chore because people were very weak, and people were lethargic and didn't want to, really didn't care what was happening anymore. And so we, we felt that we-- by doing a little work and volunteering and keeping active, it will help us survive. So that was the only choice we did there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you and your sister, I'm sure, losing weight through this ordeal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. We, we, we lost-- as soon as we arrived, I mean, because of the lack of food, the next month none of us women menstruated.

speaker: interviewer
text: That quickly?

speaker: interviewee
text: That quickly, one month later. And I always felt that it could not have been caused by just a lack of food and starvation in one month. I always felt that perhaps something was put in our food because everyone, every one of us stopped menstruating as soon as we arrived in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your health in general during that six months? How--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were weak and cold. And I was more religious than my sisters. And I refused to eat the food. And my older sister Yaffa and my Aunt Rosie kind of pushed the food into my mouth and said that it was a bigger sin to commit suicide from not eating than to eat non-kosher food. So I start eating again. If not for being with them, I probably would have died because I refused to eat the food. And there were other people who probably were religious and didn't eat the food or didn't-- needed some support and depressed. And all you had to do was touch the electric wire and be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I, I had some girlfriends who did that. They just could not stand the conditions. So it was, you know, really hard and had to be brave to want to go on. But we were-- you know, we were just generally, out of the six months we were there, I was the most sick, depressed, and weak. And I was the smallest in weight even though I was not the youngest among the three of us sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was very weak. And my mother always gave me extra food. Like at home, she always gave me the, the nice part of the milk, the fat part of the milk when you boiled it. And she always kind of pampered me because I was smaller and weaker than my other two sisters. So if not for being with them, I probably would have perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to the teacher, the pre-- the teacher you'd had in school who helped you know what selection.

speaker: interviewee
text: She ca-- she came to us because she knew that we were avoiding selections.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister did, and I was going to put it down, and I f-- I forgot the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she has it. She, she told us don't afraid to go to the selection. So we, we went. And they had many selections and, and just so many. And then they would dismiss us. And the Germans purposely wanted to separate sisters because they felt that in strength there's-- in number, there's strength. And so in some selections, we were always afraid to stand near each other because all three of us resemble each other a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in this selection, she said, don't be afraid to stand near each other. And this is-- this is a good selection. You'll be glad. You will kiss me, a different expression what you are going to do with the rest of your life for send-- for letting you go to this selection.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Germans needed 80 women for electronics factory work. And we had to line up and put our hands out, make sure we had steady hands. And we have to have good eyesight and to know German, to speak German. And we were raised with our Jewish-- our Yiddish-- was very Germanized, because my parents-- my mother was from Austro-Hungary. So our Yiddish was very Germanized. So we faked it. They asked us a few questions in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what they asked you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, basically, your name, and how old are you and just, just little basic things. So of course we were able to answer it because-- and where are you from. And so are we, we, you know, we had proper pronunciation. And we-- and we passed. And the 80 of us-- 80 of us were sent out of Auschwitz. And that's how we got out of Auschwitz to this factory. And it's similar to Schindler's list, you know, when he selected people go to work. And this was a electronics factory where we soldered the wires for the walkie talkie radios, for radios for the soldiers. And it was called Telefunken. I, I remember reading something about it later. But Telefunken was in the city, the Sudetenland. And the name of the city was Maehrisch-Weisswasser. And I think it was like in the Czechoslovakian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we ended up in this town. And we were-- we were in barracks. And we were marched to work every day. And we were under the supervision of the SS women. We had like half a dozen-- four, four SS women that were guarding us and at the factory. And we, we marched there every morning. And we worked under the supervision of the SS people.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we worked with Frenchmen. And these Frenchmen were forced laborers. They were not Jewish people, and they were not prisoners. They were from France. Instead of sending them to the army, because they were technicians and because they were electronics experts, they were needed for factory work. So instead of sending them, you know, to the war, to the front, they sent them to run factories.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these Frenchmen were forced laborers. Every morning they had to march to-- I mean they came to the factory. And they showed us-- we were like-- we were like mass production. We were doing a little work that us prisoners could do. And we were taught how to solder wires and just like assembly line work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so the Frenchmen were teaching us and working with us. And we were not allowed to communicate with them except for work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your living conditions in these barracks. There were 80 of you? We were 80 women. And we were-- there also were bunks, you know, multi-level bunks. So I think there were just two level. I kind of vaguely remember it was two levels instead of three. And we were sharing bunks. And we, we had our one uniform and blanket and very minimal clothing. And we were, you know, just stayed in [INAUDIBLE] all day. And food? And food?

speaker: interviewee
text: And food, our food was a little bit better than Auschwitz because we were working. And so it was very minimal, but, we we did get more food than we got in Auschwitz, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they-- were the guards cruel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the SS who were guarding, they lived in a separate little building. And the would come and supervise us and every now and then have inspections of the barracks to see if we did a clean. And if we violated any kind of rule, their favorite-- they would just walk around with their sticks and hit. And they were-- women almost were more vicious than men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really? Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We find that during our encounter that the, the SS women were-- seems like they, they derived more pleasure out of punishing and hitting than did sometimes the men. Because punishment was, in this particular work camp, was to shave your head again because we had our heads, hair shaved in Auschwitz. And our hair was just growing back slowly. And we were so proud of it. You know that we-- you know, we had a couple inches, three, four inches of hair. And we felt like human beings and like females again. And the SS women's biggest pride was to-- for the slightest punishment-- to shave your hair again. So they derived pleasure out of that. And just to beat us or just to, to take away privileges or take away the food or, or go be put in a separate-- locked up like-- the-- they, they were-- some of-- most of them were very vicious. Sadistic, I think that's a proper for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you worked out how many hours a day at Telefunken-- We worked-- --a typical day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We, we marched there like very early in the morning, like 6:00 in the morning. We didn't get back until 6:00 in the evening. We had a break for eating. We weren't allowed to talk to each other or communicate. We just to stay in your spot and eat your food quietly. And when we get to go to the washroom, the SS women were supervising us all over. And we had to go to-- every now and then we just had one supervising, so we could cheat a little bit because only one can look in every direction. And when we had to go to the washroom, we had to get permission to one person at a time to go to the washroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was-- how was your mental state at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at first it was bad. My mental state wasn't particularly bad because I was more depressed and I thought the war is never going to end and I'm not going to survive, and my two sisters were encouraging me. And it was-- I was-- we, we were basically we were optimistic that since we were working there and they needed our work that we would survive. And the rumors were coming all the time the war is almost at the end. And we were basically optimistic. But I was more depressed than my two sisters. And I was depressed. I was probably sick. I never knew what happened to me, but I fainted one day at, at the factory. And because I was a good worker, the SS woman took me to, to a-- it was like a, a car, a ambulance and let me stay there for three days to get better because I was a good worker, and they needed me. And I stayed in this ambulance for like three days and came back to the barrack. But I was-- just used to tell my sister that I feel like actually my heart hurts.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I know you, you had a very special experience there. Tell us about Pierre and how that whole thing started.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the-- in the radio factory, after-- we were there a very short time. I think it was just a few days or a week. I have to check my little diary to see how soon after we got to the factory. The Frenchman were working. And this one Frenchman Pierre motioned to me that he's going to put something down near the equipment, to look for it. So when the SS women weren't looking, I went there, and I picked it up. And it was just a piece of paper that said look for this spot tomorrow. I will be leave you with some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took the paper and stuck it in my bosom. And when got back to the-- we went back in the barracks, some of the women that were with us were French women. So I was able to have her read the note, what it said because I couldn't speak French. And so she told me to look for this spot. Tomorrow I will bring you some food. So sure enough, the next day, behind this equipment I-- you know, when the SS women weren't looking, I went there. And there was two pieces of bread, and, and which was something very special. And it had butter on it, and honey, and layer on honey and apple in it, things that we haven't had since he left home. So I got this bread. And after that he sent me a note that he was going to send me paper and pencil so that I could write letters to him. And he, he did. He took-- he gave me a pencil and paper. And I started writing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every day he would bring me packages and, you know, just a little food. And I couldn't wait to get back to our barrack. And then we posted guards at the door so that the SS women didn't drop in on us. And we shared the food. And he was kind of encouragement for me to go on and to get these notes. I felt like a human being again, and like a girl again. I started combing my hair and watching, trying to see in the mirror. Not a mirror, but we just had to find metal, things that reflected to see what we looked like. So that was-- that was a big boost to my-- in my mental state to have him bring these foods.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you told me in the pre-interview that he gave you paper and pencil, which you've just said. Can you tell us about the diary? I would like you to read-- tell us about the little diary that you wrote for your birthday. And can you read us some excerpts?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. It was-- it was April 19, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my 18th birthday. And I wrote-- and I wanted to remember that day, so Pierre gave me a little piece of paper. And I folded. And I cut it immediately into this-- the diary was just this big, and, you know, just about an inch and a half. With string that we had in the factory, I, I tied the pages together with the string. And I made a button from lead with my prisoner number. So I attached that to the diary. And I was writing in my little Hungarian tiny words so that-- letters, so I could squeeze a lot into this paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you read us part of it? This is just excerpts.

speaker: interviewee
text: Excerpts, right. This, this-- I'm preparing-- this the date on this. It was April 19, 1945, in Maehrisch-Weisswasser. And that's where our factory was. I'm preparing for myself this tiny notebook as a present for my 18th birthday. When I will be living as free a person again, I shall want to remember some of the rare, pleasant moments that occurred during my difficult camp life. I shall want to remember some of the good deeds that people who worked with us in the electronics factory performed. These were Frenchmen, not of the Jewish faith. They were forced laborers who had to work for the Germans during the day. But their job at the factory was done at the end of each day. They were free to leave. We girls were prisoners of the Germans. We are guarded all the time, and at the end of the day, we're marched back to our camp. I want to write down the dates of all the present events so that never in my life will I-- will I forget them. On December 28, 1944, I met-- met, quotation marks-- the Frenchman named Pierre. Every day, he left a package of food for me. For three months, he faithfully gave me packages without missing on a single day. On Saturdays, he even enclosed more food to last me for Sunday also when the factory was closed. He lifted my spirits to see that nice people still existed, especially people who were not of the Jewish faith. In Auschwitz I almost fell apart. I lost a lot weight. I was sick all the time. I was terribly depressed. I didn't want to live. I didn't want to eat. I actually felt that my heart hurt. And then, Pierre became my friend. Perhaps I owe him that I am still alive in this camp. And that I would be able to witness liberation day. Never, but never will I ever forget him. And I, I, I did. I never forgot him. After we were liberated, he wanted me to go with him. And as a-- and of course, I did not want to go because he was not Jewish. And I didn't feel like I survived the war and then to give up my faith, and I want to stay with my sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as a token of friendship, he gave me this ring. And I never take it off. This, this, this was-- even, even when I-- I just have it on all the time. Harold doesn't mind. Just only time I take it off is when I have surgery because it represents my survival because of Pierre bringing the packages and also the encouragement and-- Hope.

speaker: interviewee
text: The hope, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Liberation day was very unusual because we heard the war was coming to an end. And the American army was approaching, and the Russian army was approaching.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was probably May of 1945. I don't have the date. I probably could get it from some friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's close enough. That's good.

speaker: interviewee
text: But May. So the American army coming and the German army was coming-- I mean the Russian army. And we were hoping it would end.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one morning, we were in the barracks. And every morning the, the alarm, you know, would go off. The, the siren would go off when you had to get up, you know, time to get up and, and march to their factory. So this day, we had-- we didn't hear any alarms going off. So we realized something special happened. So we walked out from the barracks. And we saw the gate was open. And the SS women escaped. They, they-- you know, they all escaped. So we knew something happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's why we-- and then the Frenchman came to our barracks and told us that the war-- the war ended and the, the Germans have run off, and we were liberated and to hide. And they took us from, from our camp and took us to private homes to hide because they, they were afraid. They said that when the Russian army passes through, they rape women and girls, and just to be scared and not to be around. So to wait until they pass through towns.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- they hid us, the Frenchman. Some of the other French-- other workers did meet their Frenchman. They did develop friendships after, after the-- after we were liberated and went with them. But the Frenchman put us up in, in the homes, abandoned homes. Was Pierre one those--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pierre--

speaker: interviewer
text: --who helped hide you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pierre-- no Pierre brought us to the-- to this home and showed us where and brought food.

speaker: interviewer
text: All three of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: All three of us, yes-- six of us. We were six of us in the special home. And we, we stayed there. And he brought food. And that's where he game me pictures. And that's when he gave me-- you know, when we said goodbye. But a couple of women did go with the Frenchman. And we, we just had to find their way back to our homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in these French homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in their homes-- we were stayed-- we stayed about a week. And then we start going close-- in the direction of our home to go back to Kolozsvar. So we just try to find all means of transportation to try and, and inch our way back to, to our home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and is that what you indeed did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we, we, we tried to hitch hike and get by wagon and bicycle and trains. And one train ride, I particularly remember. The Russians soldiers got on the train. And they were approaching my sister, Miriam. And the train was going slowly, so she jumped off. And then we, we jumped off after that because we wanted to be together because we were scared of the-- the Russian soldiers were always collecting watches. And sometime when they would reach out, they would have like half a dozen watches on their arm. To, to-- that was one of the favorite things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we slowly, we went back. It took us about two weeks. And you didn't have any money?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have money. We, we, we just hand me downs. I mean, just the, the Frenchman gave us some. And then the local people gave us some food. We did not have none.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you-- were you in touch with any of the relief agencies yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point, no, no. Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you made your way back home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it took about two weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: About two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you got back to your--

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got back to our home, we, we found different people living in our house, and we-- you know, Gentile people. And when we came back, one person in the house commented immediately. He said, you are back. We thought the Germans killed all of you. And it was very unexpected we came back. So we, we went to a shelter, you know, that they had for the survivors. And we start thinking what to do and, you know, to find-- we knew we can't stay there because we were not wanted in Kolozsvar.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did know. How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just, just from the, the, the reception we received that by saying that we thought that the Germans killed all of you. And, and people were very nervous and annoyed. And they weren't going to give us any help. And the only help that we did-- my sis-- my oldest sister did find was with the-- my, my-- her fiance's parents owned a flour mill. And before they left to camp, they turned the flour mill over to this Gentile family. And when my, my future brother-in-law, my sister's fiance, survived and came back and this family turned the flour mill back to him and returned everything. So they had a house--

speaker: interviewer
text: The family turned the flour mill back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to my, my brother-in-law, my sister's fiance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now was that while y'all were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: While we were-- while we were in Cluj-- while we were in Kolozsvar, we left. But my sister stayed behind because she heard that her fiance survived. So she didn't want to go with us to try and find our way for a new home in Palestine because she said my fiance survived. I'm staying. So we parted.

speaker: interviewer
text: And had you heard anything at this point about your mother, your father, your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The only thing we heard from other people that the-- my-- the older people were killed, and forget it. You're never going to see them. And my father, we did hear subsequently from people and my brother that-- not when we were in Kolozsvar. Not when-- later on, we find out that my brother and father were together in camp. And they were in six different camps. And the last camp was Dachau. And that camp was liberated by the American army. And my father died just a week before liberation. My brother survived, was liberated by the American army. The Red Cross picked him up and took him to a hospital. He weighed about 80 pounds. And he--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did that know that at first?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not know. When we got back to Cluj, we did not know anything where anybody was. Only thing we did was constantly ask each other, do you know this person? Do you know that person? Have you heard what happened? And then slowly people would come and say, well, yes, I was with this person and died. Or I was with this person and, and was in this camp with me. Or just constantly every list and every information, just trying to get in touch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back a minute to when you found that your house had been taken over after you left. Did they take over all of your possessions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your neighbors save anything? Did they save anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything in the house was gone. I mean, our furniture, some of it was still there. But all our personal mementos and everything like that, picture, everything was gone. But we recognized, you know, like the table. And then a chifferobe, a big, you know, wardrobe, we recognized that. And that was there. And they didn't know anything about. This is ours. We bought this. Forget it. This is ours. And the only thing that was left, my sister went back to a neighbor who-- a Gentile person-- who before we were taken-- weeks before we were taken-- my sister was studying to be a seamstress. And she made these dresses, three dresses, herself. And she said, I would hate for this to be lost because I worked so hard. I mean, it was so beautiful. I still remember what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she took that, the three dresses, and the photo album because she was very active in the Zionist youth group. And she had all these pictures. And so she decided, OK, I'm taking this photo album and bring that to the neighbor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we came back, we found it, that she had the photo album and the three dresses. So that's about the only thing that we have left from before the war. The pictures, and her-- it was her photo album. So it doesn't have many pictures of us in it because first of all, we didn't take many pictures because my parents wouldn't let us take pictures anyway. But my sister in the Zionist youth group did take pictures and had a lot of her group, her friends in it. And I have one picture in it with my sister. I'm probably 14, sitting in the park reading with my sister, Yaffa. And that's the only picture that I have of my early childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're-- how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we went been back to Cluj?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed about, I would say, about two or three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two or three weeks. And you said there was a shelter there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we, we had shelter. And we were orphans of the war and survivors. And we were put in the shelter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that shelter was run by the city? By the government? By the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: That shelter, I don't know who it was run by. Later on, the shelters where run by the Jewish agency and help from overseas. And, and we-- this was called a shelter. And later on they were called DP camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's when they were run by the Jewish agency. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop for a minute.
